name: Trisirobhairava-Tantra (position-entry)
dates_notes: This is a position-entry, not an author-entry. The Triśirobhairava-Tantra is anonymous and largely lost as a continuous text; what survives is a large body of citations and paraphrases in Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* and Jayaratha's *Tantrāloka-Viveka*, together with related material in other Kashmirian commentarial sources. The text is internally datable to the seventh–ninth centuries CE on the basis of its position as a developed Bhairava-tantra cited by Abhinavagupta as established scriptural authority (Sanderson; Hatley).

lineage_polemical.note: The Triśirobhairava-Tantra is one of the principal source-tantras Abhinavagupta cites in the *Tantrāloka*, alongside the Mālinī-Vijaya and the Siddha-Yogeśvarī-Mata. The relation is engaged-from-within: Abhinavagupta is doing the philosophy of the source-tantra, not refuting it.

core_thesis: The *Triśirobhairava-Tantra* is one of the principal Bhairava-tantras of the *Vidyā-pīṭha* — the section of the Śaiva canon devoted to the goddess-centered tantras — and one of the principal source-texts cited by Abhinavagupta as scriptural authority for the Trika. The tantra is named for the three-headed (*tri-śiras*) Bhairava and articulates the three-fold (*trika*) structure of supreme consciousness as Śiva, Śakti, and Nara (or as the three goddesses Parā, Parāparā, and Aparā) in a developed Bhairava-tantra ritual and metaphysical programme. The work is largely lost as a continuous text but is reconstructable from the extensive citation chain in Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* and Jayaratha's *Tantrāloka-Viveka*. Its principal contributions are three. First, the *trika*-articulation of supreme consciousness into the three goddesses Parā (the supreme), Parāparā (the supreme-and-non-supreme), and Aparā (the non-supreme) — a doctrine that gives the Trika its name and that Abhinavagupta develops as the structural foundation of the school. Second, the *kuṇḍalinī*-and-*cakra* yoga articulated as the rising of consciousness through the three-fold articulation of the goddess. Third, the ritual programme of *yāga* (sacrifice), *mantra*, and *dīkṣā* (initiation) in the goddess-centered Vidyā-pīṭha mode. The text's significance is that Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* cites it more often than any source other than the *Mālinī-Vijaya*; without the Triśirobhairava, the developed Trika is incomplete.

engaged_works.title: Trisirobhairava-Tantra
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Anonymous; dated by Abhinavagupta's tenth-century treatment as established scriptural authority and by the developed character of its Bhairava-tantra apparatus.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Anonymous; ascribed by tradition to Bhairava revelation. The text is largely lost as a continuous source and is principally reconstructable from the citation chain in Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* and Jayaratha's *Tantrāloka-Viveka*.

ascription_evidence.description: Sanderson's essays on the Śaiva canon; Hatley's essays on the Vidyā-pīṭha; the citation analysis in modern scholarship on the Tantrāloka.

engaged_works.summary: A largely lost Bhairava-tantra of the Vidyā-pīṭha, reconstructable from the extensive citation chain in Abhinavagupta's *Tantrāloka* and Jayaratha's *Tantrāloka-Viveka*. The work articulates the *trika*-structure of supreme consciousness as Śiva-Śakti-Nara or as the three goddesses Parā, Parāparā, and Aparā; the *kuṇḍalinī*-and-*cakra* yoga in the three-fold articulation; and the goddess-centered Vidyā-pīṭha ritual programme of *yāga*, *mantra*, and *dīkṣā*. The text is one of the principal source-tantras of the Trika and the document Abhinavagupta cites second most often (after the *Mālinī-Vijaya*) as scriptural authority.

reception_notes: The Triśirobhairava-Tantra is one of the principal source-tantras of the Trika as Abhinavagupta presents it. The work is largely lost but its citations in the *Tantrāloka* and *Tantrāloka-Viveka* are extensive enough that modern scholarship (Sanderson; Hatley) treats it as a reconstructable position even in the absence of a continuous text. **None of the modern scholars' works is on disk in our corpus; all cited via field-standard summary.**
